jokes.text: Something's coming this way, sergeant. Something big! Don't worry, men! It's time for the USA to win a war.
jokes.setup: Military preparing for major battle with patriotic confidence
jokes.punchline: Dark irony about America's war record
jokes.explanation: Sharp dark humor about military history, economical setup-punchline structure

jokes.text: My God, it's a stapler!
jokes.punchline: Reveals trivial office supply as the 'threat'
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy - builds military tension then deflates with mundane object

jokes.text: Today? Of course I know. Happy birthday... / It's not my birthday, Seymour. You know I'm a Sagittarius.
jokes.punchline: Chalmers correcting with astrological sign reference
jokes.explanation: Classic Skinner awkwardness, good character integration but predictable structure

jokes.text: Really? I'm a Libra. We're very compatible. / Skinner, you can be gay on your own time.
jokes.setup: Skinner suggesting romantic compatibility through astrology
jokes.punchline: Chalmers' blunt dismissal
jokes.explanation: Sharp character comedy with Chalmers' trademark bluntness cutting through Skinner's obliviousness

jokes.text: I expect it to be like cats and dogs in here. / Sir, the school has a strict policy against animals. I assume you're speaking hypothetically about cats and dogs?
jokes.punchline: Skinner's literal interpretation of the metaphor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner characterization - his bureaucratic literalness creates the humor

jokes.text: Everyone loves gum machines. So why not a machine with okra soup gel?
jokes.setup: Logical premise about popular gum machines
jokes.punchline: Bizarre leap to disgusting okra soup gel
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic - starts reasonable then veers into disgusting territory

jokes.text: Does your school nurse treat burns? / No. / Couldn't be simpler!
jokes.punchline: Vendor's enthusiasm about lack of burn treatment
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - vendor sees lack of medical care as selling point

jokes.text: Kids want a snack that skateboards, doesn't clean its room, and hates homework. That's why we created Scammer and Z-Dog Scarfables!
jokes.setup: Marketing logic about what kids want
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizing snacks with rebellious teen traits
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of marketing anthropomorphizing products with 'attitude'

jokes.text: Hip-Hop slushies! Yeah, I saw those when my usual grocery store was on strike.
jokes.punchline: Skinner's mundane reason for knowing trendy product
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Skinner's accidental hipness through mundane circumstances

jokes.text: It's like a school fundraiser wrapped in slang!
jokes.punchline: Chalmers seeing through the marketing veneer
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about corporate manipulation disguised as youth culture

jokes.text: And a subliminal neuro-jammer disrupts the children's judgment.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of mind control technology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - vendor casually admitting to manipulating children's minds

jokes.text: Very well. I assume these snacks are nutritious? / That's not our concern. The point is half the money goes to the school.
jokes.punchline: Vendor's blunt dismissal of health concerns
jokes.explanation: Good corporate cynicism - profit over health presented matter-of-factly

jokes.text: Look at the ingredients! Hydrogenated oil... monoxide poison, weaponized plutonium?
jokes.punchline: Escalation to impossible nuclear weapons material
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from real concerning ingredients to absurd nuclear material

jokes.text: Attention naive masses! These new snacks are filled with... processed sugar, industrial byproducts, and fattening acids!
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to warn other students
jokes.punchline: Calling classmates 'naive masses' while lecturing
jokes.explanation: Classic Lisa character trait - righteous but condescending activism

jokes.text: Don't hate the flavors. Participate! / The machine is right. / Don't be a victim. Swallow snacks! / It's fun to obey the machine.
jokes.punchline: Students mindlessly agreeing to obey machine
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dystopian humor - children enthusiastically embracing machine control

jokes.text: Good for you! You're 5 minutes away from a fantastic beverage or snack.
jokes.setup: Machine congratulating student for using it
jokes.punchline: Creates artificial sense of achievement for basic purchase
jokes.explanation: Satirizes manipulative marketing that treats consumption as accomplishment

jokes.text: I'm out of money! Willie, want to buy my skateboard? / Why not? For once, everyone will look at Willie!
jokes.setup: Bart desperate to sell skateboard for snack money
jokes.punchline: Willie's pathetic excitement about getting attention
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie characterization - his desperate need for attention creates sympathy and humor

jokes.text: Look at me, I'm an American boy!
jokes.punchline: His attempt at American youth culture
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Willie's attempt to fit in with American youth culture

jokes.text: Stairs! / No! I don't... / Okay, I'm fine. / No! I spoke too soon! / Better now.
jokes.punchline: Multiple false recoveries and injuries
jokes.explanation: Classic slapstick escalation - multiple injuries with false recoveries

jokes.text: What are you looking at, bastards!
jokes.punchline: His angry outburst at onlookers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie - his embarrassment turned to Scottish anger is pure character

jokes.text: Eat some. / No thanks. The vending machine at school feeds me now. / So I've been replaced by a machine?
jokes.punchline: Homer's existential crisis about being replaced
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Homer's wounded pride about being outperformed by machine

jokes.text: Marge-bot ready for some loving!
jokes.punchline: Homer's fantasy robot Marge
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - his simple mind immediately imagines robot replacement for everything

jokes.text: Why did I give her a weapon?
jokes.punchline: Homer's regret about arming his fantasy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - creates problem then questions his own poor planning

jokes.text: My heart... hurts so much. Like it's caught in a vise! / My little boy is in love. / I think he's having a heart attack!
jokes.punchline: Marge's romantic interpretation vs Homer's medical accuracy
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast - Marge's romantic optimism vs Homer's blunt realism

jokes.text: Doctor, little kids shouldn't have heart attacks. They should scrape their knees and poke their eyes out and get strangled by cats.
jokes.setup: Marge expressing concern about children's health
jokes.punchline: Her casual list of acceptable childhood injuries
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - Marge's motherly concern paired with casual violence

jokes.text: These dark spots are chocolates. / His liver looks healthy. / That's a wafer.
jokes.punchline: X-rays showing actual candy instead of organs
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual absurdity - Bart so full of junk food it shows on medical imaging

jokes.text: I won't bury another patient! / Doctor, you're a pediatrician! / Yes, but my mind is elsewhere this year.
jokes.setup: Doctor's dramatic declaration about patient mortality
jokes.punchline: Revelation he's distracted from pediatric work
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about medical malpractice and doctor distraction

jokes.text: I won't bury another patient! Doctor, you're a pediatrician!
jokes.setup: Doctor making dramatic statement about patient mortality
jokes.punchline: Reminder that he treats children, making statement absurd
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about children's doctor having mortality issues

jokes.text: I SLEEP WITH YOUR WIFE
jokes.setup: Bart doing stress test on exercise machine
jokes.punchline: Machine displaying inappropriate message during medical test
jokes.explanation: Brilliant unexpected visual gag - exercise machine displaying personal insult during medical test

jokes.text: Bart, how can you laugh at this? You just had a heart attack. / Yes, but what doesn't kill me makes me stronger.
jokes.setup: Marge concerned about Bart's casual attitude
jokes.punchline: Bart misapplying inspirational quote to heart attack
jokes.explanation: Good ironic misuse of motivational quote in completely inappropriate context

jokes.text: Those were a lot of words. I need some energy.
jokes.punchline: Immediately needing sugar fix after speaking
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency - even philosophical moments trigger sugar craving

jokes.text: Hello kids! Heart disease can strike anyone. Like clowns who are alcoholics and smokers. That's why I had the doctor give me a zipper.
jokes.setup: Krusty giving health message to children
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of multiple vices and surgical zipper
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty - inappropriate health PSA while admitting to unhealthy lifestyle

jokes.text: Hello kids! Heart disease can strike anyone. Like clowns who are alcoholics and smokers. That's why I had the doctor install a zipper.
jokes.setup: Krusty giving health message to children
jokes.punchline: Uses himself as bad example and reveals bizarre medical modification
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty character moment - inappropriate health messaging with personal confession

jokes.text: The zipper got stuck. Should have gone with buttons.
jokes.punchline: Immediate practical problem with his solution
jokes.explanation: Great follow-through - Krusty's medical innovation immediately fails

jokes.text: Disgusting! From what it looks like, this cheese has already been eaten.
jokes.punchline: Bart's disgusted description of natural cheese appearance
jokes.explanation: Good child's reaction to unfamiliar healthy food - accurate kid perspective

jokes.text: When people asked how you were, I'd say: At least he has his health. / Now you have nothing.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining past pride in Bart's health
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment of Bart's current worthlessness
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - Marge's motherly disappointment expressed with brutal honesty

jokes.text: It's graffiti you can eat! / Get well, son.
jokes.setup: Marge presenting vegetable art to sick Bart
jokes.punchline: Homer's formal farewell implying Bart won't recover
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Marge's creative effort vs Homer's pessimistic farewell

jokes.text: Today only, free milkshakes for ten-year-old boys. / Drink your weight in milkshake and win a milkshake!
jokes.punchline: Impossible consumption challenge with minimal reward
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist marketing - impossible challenge with worthless prize

jokes.text: Intervention! / What the hell? / Child, we're here to help you with... / Which addiction are we dealing with? / Gluttony.
jokes.punchline: Having to specify which of multiple addictions they're addressing
jokes.explanation: Good family dynamic - multiple addictions requiring clarification

jokes.text: So tired that the only thing that picks me up is smoking. / The only thing it does is bring me back to normal.
jokes.punchline: Admission that addiction only maintains baseline function
jokes.explanation: Excellent addiction psychology - honest assessment of dependency vs enhancement

jokes.text: eat my shorts, losers!
jokes.punchline: Classic Bart catchphrase as escape line
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - classic Bart catchphrase in appropriate rebellious context

jokes.text: Guess how old I am. Come on, guess. / 47? / It hurts when people say higher. Still stings a little.
jokes.setup: Camp director asking age guessing game
jokes.punchline: Hurt feelings over age estimate being too high
jokes.explanation: Good character vanity moment - military tough guy sensitive about age

jokes.text: Maybe you mean 'tough love'? / Tough love? Of course, that never made sense. The guy who sold it to me lied. Stanley.
jokes.punchline: Camp director bought concept from salesman named Stanley
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - treating 'tough love' as purchasable commodity from salesman

jokes.text: Tough love? Of course, that never made sense. The guy who sold it to me lied. Stanley.
jokes.setup: Correcting his 'tough luck' to 'tough love' philosophy
jokes.punchline: Reveals he bought concept from someone named Stanley
jokes.explanation: Absurd idea that parenting philosophy was a purchased product

jokes.text: Thank you for the correction. It's part of honesty. I'll leave you with advice: Become emotionally dead. To your body. Make lots of friends.
jokes.punchline: Contradictory advice about emotional death and friendship
jokes.explanation: Self-contradictory life advice from confused authority figure

jokes.text: Kent Brockman, Channel 6 News. I ate while others did weather and sports. / Your fat is starting to spill into Channels 5 and 7. I hope you're not getting three salaries.
jokes.punchline: Fat literally expanding into other TV channels
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual metaphor - obesity expanding across TV channels like actual fat spillover

jokes.text: Kent Brockman, from Channel 6 News. I ate while others reported weather and sports. Your fat has started spilling into channels 5 and 7. Hope you're not getting three salaries.
jokes.setup: News anchor explaining how he got fat
jokes.punchline: Instructor's comment about fat literally affecting other TV channels
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual metaphor of obesity affecting TV broadcast channels

jokes.text: You old fatso! Can you cut back on your makeup expenses?
jokes.setup: Family discussing expensive fat camp costs
jokes.punchline: Marge's desperate cost-cutting suggestion about makeup
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Marge's financial desperation leading to petty criticism

jokes.text: I already use crayons for lipstick and coal for eyeliner. / So that's where all my coal went.
jokes.setup: Marge defending her frugal makeup choices
jokes.punchline: Homer solving mystery of missing coal
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Homer's simple problem-solving and Marge's extreme frugality

jokes.text: So that's where all my charcoal went.
jokes.setup: Learning Marge uses charcoal for makeup
jokes.punchline: Finally solves mystery of missing charcoal
jokes.explanation: Homer's self-centered concern about his missing supplies

jokes.text: $50? And who's going to pay for all the water used in your baths and showers and washing clothes? / We don't do that, no problem.
jokes.setup: Negotiating hostel rates with concerns about utilities
jokes.punchline: Germans casually admitting to poor hygiene
jokes.explanation: Mild stereotype humor about European backpackers and hygiene

jokes.text: While you put the spoon in the ice cream, tell it you hate it. Talk to it. What did you call me? What did the vanilla say? No, I'm not that old!
jokes.setup: Therapy exercise talking to ice cream
jokes.punchline: Director getting defensive about imaginary ice cream insults
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist therapy - director projecting insecurities onto food therapy

jokes.text: German tourists? You see, every family needs extra income... when they send a child like you to a place like mine.
jokes.setup: Showing Bart how his family is coping financially
jokes.punchline: Reveals parents forced into hosting strangers to pay for his treatment
jokes.explanation: Guilt manipulation showing child's financial burden on family

jokes.text: They can't see or hear us, right? / We're not the Ghost of Christmas Past. They can see us. If I were invisible would I waste time wearing these clothes?
jokes.setup: Bart asking about invisibility while spying
jokes.punchline: Spangler's logical fashion-based proof of visibility
jokes.explanation: Good logical humor - using fashion choices to prove visibility

jokes.text: So this is the little fatty eating strudel... who bankrupted them with his expensive treatments. Little fatty eating strudel. You should think about that.
jokes.setup: German tourist describing Bart to his parents
jokes.punchline: Brutal characterization as 'little fatty eating strudel'
jokes.explanation: Harsh outside perspective on Bart's situation and impact on family

jokes.text: Problem #35 with America: No free healthcare. #36: No metric system. What are you, living in Charlemagne's time? Answer me. Answer me now.
jokes.punchline: Escalating to demanding immediate answers about historical references
jokes.explanation: Good cultural criticism escalating to demanding historical accountability

jokes.text: Here's your tip, baldy. If you want it, dance and sing.
jokes.punchline: Humiliating conditions for small payment
jokes.explanation: Standard humiliation humor - tourist treating Homer like street performer

jokes.text: Great! More, more! They want me to do the rest. I hope he knows it.
jokes.punchline: Marge hoping Homer knows more of whatever he's performing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Marge's concerned support of Homer's humiliation

jokes.text: I'm to blame for everything. I'll never eat junk food again. I swear it.
jokes.punchline: Finally accepts responsibility and swears off junk food
jokes.explanation: Character growth moment as Bart recognizes consequences of his actions

jokes.text: Bart, what are you doing here? I learned my lesson. But... you're still fat.
jokes.setup: Bart returning home claiming to have learned his lesson
jokes.punchline: Homer's blunt observation that he's still overweight
jokes.explanation: Homer's practical observation undermining Bart's claimed transformation

jokes.text: When I went against the system, money came out. My boy! I know we didn't earn this honestly, but we can... Use it to kick them out? Yes.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining how he got money from vending machine
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately suggesting using illegally obtained money to solve problems
jokes.explanation: Homer's immediate acceptance of using questionably obtained money

jokes.text: Sorry, I think I left my belt here. No, you didn't leave it. Don't tell me I didn't leave it. This is my lucky belt. Big beautiful story.
jokes.setup: Spangler claiming he left a belt at Simpson house
jokes.punchline: Insists on non-existent belt with supposed significance
jokes.explanation: Spangler's delusional insistence about imaginary personal property

jokes.text: Why are you all looking at me? / I'm not too fat. I'm alive, aren't I?
jokes.setup: Family suggesting Homer needs fat camp
jokes.punchline: Homer's defensive logic using survival as fitness proof
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - using minimal survival as proof of health

jokes.text: Why are you all looking at me? I'm not too fat. I'm alive, aren't I?
jokes.setup: Family suggesting Homer use the weight loss program
jokes.punchline: Homer's defensive response using survival as health metric
jokes.explanation: Homer's low standards for health and fitness assessment

jokes.text: We can decide when we're standing naked over the bed. / My God, that's why you eat.
jokes.punchline: Spangler's psychological insight about Homer's eating motivation
jokes.explanation: Good uncomfortable humor linking sexual awkwardness to food addiction

jokes.text: We'll share a room. Where will I sleep? We can decide that when we're standing naked over the bed.
jokes.setup: Discussing sleeping arrangements during road trip
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate suggestion about naked bed-sharing decision
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable sexual innuendo creating awkward moment

jokes.text: Give me half. / I don't want to. / Just the cheese, not the meat. I want the meat. Take a bite. / Don't cut it. Give it all to me. You'll eat later. / You're a selfish pig. / Since I smelled it, I have to eat it. / But it's mine. / I'm driving, I'll kill us. / Fine, I'd rather die.
jokes.punchline: Escalation to choosing death over sharing food
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - food fight escalating to life-or-death ultimatum with perfect absurdist logic
